_comment_overview: 3-Way Task-Specific Aggregation: The hierarchical confounder extractors use 3 learnable queries to aggregate K confounder representations. For DragonNet: propensity, Y0 (outcome under control), Y1 (outcome under treatment). For R-Learner: propensity, m(X) (marginal outcome), tau(X) (treatment effect). This allows the model to learn which confounders are prognostic vs treatment effect modifiers.
_comment_dimensionality: With K latent confounders of dimension D, the 3-way aggregation produces 3 task-specific representations of dimension D each, concatenated to 3*D before the final MLP. For example: K=8, D=128 -> 3*128=384 instead of K*D=1024. This reduces dimensionality while allowing task specialization.

architecture._comment_model_type: Choose 'rlearner' or 'dragonnet'. R-Learner uses propensity/outcome/tau queries. DragonNet uses propensity/y0/y1 queries. R-Learner is recommended when treatment effect heterogeneity is the primary focus.
architecture._comment_gru_mode: Enable GRU-based hierarchical extraction. All parameters (embeddings, GRU, attention, latent confounders, task queries) learn together via causal objective.
architecture._comment_3way_aggregation: The 3-way task-specific aggregation uses 3 learnable query vectors: For R-Learner: (1) propensity query aggregates confounders for P(T=1|X), (2) outcome query for E[Y|X], (3) tau query for E[Y(1)-Y(0)|X]. For DragonNet: (1) propensity, (2) y0 query for P(Y=1|T=0,X), (3) y1 query for P(Y=1|T=1,X). This allows the model to weight confounders differently for predicting treatment assignment vs outcomes.
architecture._comment_latents: Number of learnable latent confounder vectors (K). Each learns to attend to different aspects of the text. The 3-way aggregation then selects which confounders matter for each task.
architecture._comment_gru_embedding: Word embedding dimension for the learnable vocabulary
architecture._comment_gru_hidden: GRU hidden state dimension per direction (total = 2x if bidirectional)
architecture._comment_gru_layers: Number of stacked GRU layers (1-2 recommended)
architecture._comment_gru_bidirectional: Use bidirectional GRU (recommended for better context)
architecture._comment_gru_dropout: Dropout rate within GRU layers (only applies if num_layers > 1)
architecture._comment_gru_vocab: Maximum vocabulary size. Words beyond this are mapped to UNK.
architecture._comment_gru_min_freq: Minimum word frequency for vocabulary inclusion. Lower values keep more rare words.
architecture._comment_gru_max_sentence_length: Maximum tokens per sentence. Longer sentences are truncated.
architecture._comment_value_dim: Output dimension D of each confounder. With 3-way aggregation, the final representation is 3*D.
architecture._comment_max_sentences: Maximum sentences to process per document. Longer documents are truncated.
architecture._comment_attention_heads: Number of attention heads for token-level cross-attention within sentences
architecture._comment_sparse: Sparse attention forces each confounder to focus on few sentences instead of spreading attention everywhere. Recommended for interpretability.
architecture._comment_sparse_methods: Options: 'entmax' (soft sparsity, recommended), 'topk' (hard top-k selection), 'softmax' (no sparsity)
architecture._comment_topk: Only used when sparse_method='topk'. Number of sentences each confounder can attend to.

training._comment_gamma: Weight for R-learner loss. Only used when model_type='rlearner'. Higher values emphasize treatment effect learning.

applied_inference._comment_interpretability: The 3-way aggregation provides interpretability: examine which confounders have high weights for propensity vs outcome/tau. Confounders with high propensity weights but low outcome weights are likely treatment assignment factors. Confounders with high tau weights (R-Learner) or different y0/y1 weights (DragonNet) are treatment effect modifiers.

_comment_dragonnet_variant: To use DragonNet instead of R-Learner, change model_type to 'dragonnet'. DragonNet uses y0/y1 queries to aggregate confounders separately for potential outcomes under control and treatment. This is useful when you want to examine which confounders affect outcomes differently under treatment vs control.
_comment_key_benefits: Key benefits of 3-way aggregation: (1) Dimensionality reduction from K*D to 3*D, (2) Task-specific confounder weighting, (3) Interpretability - examine which confounders drive each prediction task, (4) Better gradient flow - each task query gets direct gradients
